program: Business English — Executive Communication for International Leadership
hub_subtitle: Business English Executivo para Reuniões Internacionais e Liderança

lesson.menu_desc: Reading a written business case and separating fact from opinion -- cleft sentences and emphasis (It was ... that ...; What ... is ...; The reason ... is that ...) -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 -- The Case for the Deal -- Making an Argument with Emphasis
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Izabel Cristina de Pieri | Aula 14 -- The Case for the Deal

listenings.text: Izabel, it is Nathan from the board, with my read on your memo before we vote. Let me be direct about what convinced me. What makes the case compelling is not the excitement; it is the logic. It was the speed argument that landed hardest for me: acquiring the technology puts us in the medical market a year sooner than building it ourselves. That is the bottom line. I also liked that you did not hide the objections. The main one is price, and you were right not to take their revenue figures at face value; an independent audit will substantiate them. There is one caveat I want on the record. What worries me is integration, because a clash of engineering cultures could slow both teams. But the reason I will vote yes is that the merits outweigh the risks. The case, honestly, is close to watertight. Call me before the meeting.

Good morning, Izabel. It is Claire, your analyst, with a quick note on how the board will read the memo. My job is to separate fact from opinion, so let me flag both. The facts are solid. It is the proprietary technology that gives Nordic Sense its edge, and that is documented. But watch one assertion. The claim that a bigger rival will simply copy the design rests on a bias toward size, and I would rebut it directly. What the record actually shows is the opposite: it was reliability, not scale, that won your own clients. My advice is to lead with the strongest counterargument, not to bury it. What persuades a sceptical board is a case that answers its own objections. The reason your memo works is that it does exactly that. Keep the caveat about integration visible and honest. Talk soon.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: so convincing that it demands attention
the strong points or advantages of something
a reason someone gives for disagreeing
to support a claim with solid evidence
a confident statement presented as true
a tendency that unfairly distorts judgment
to argue against a point and disprove it
so strong that it has no weakness

paras: This memo makes the case for acquiring Nordic Sense. What makes the proposal compelling is speed: it is their proprietary sensor technology that would put AVRT in the medical market a full year sooner than building the same capability in-house. That is the bottom line. The merits are clear on paper, but a strong case must also substantiate its claims and answer the obvious objections, not just assert them.

The first objection is price. It is the valuation, not the idea, that worries the CFO, and that concern is fair. We should not take the founders' revenue figures at face value; an independent audit will substantiate them before any number is agreed. A second caveat is integration. What could slow both teams down is a clash of engineering cultures, so the plan sets aside a full quarter to manage it.

One counterargument deserves a direct rebuttal. Some directors assume that a larger rival will simply copy the technology. That assertion rests on a bias toward size. What the record actually shows is the opposite: it was reliability, not scale, that won AVRT its own clients. The reason we recommend green-lighting the deal is that the upside is real and the downside is manageable. Read fairly, the case is close to watertight.
reading.source: Adapted from an internal AVRT board memo

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the memo?
choices: AVRT should reject the Nordic Sense deal because it is far too risky.
A written case for acquiring Nordic Sense: why it is compelling, the objections and caveats it answers, and the recommendation.
A summary of AVRT's quarterly sales figures for the board.

items: The memo argues in favour of acquiring Nordic Sense.
t
'This memo makes the case for acquiring Nordic Sense.'
The writer says the founders' revenue figures should be accepted at face value.
f
'We should not take the founders' revenue figures at face value; an independent audit will substantiate them.'
The main objection raised in the memo is the price, or valuation.
t
'The first objection is price. It is the valuation... that worries the CFO.'
The memo concludes that a larger rival will certainly copy the technology.
f
'That assertion rests on a bias toward size. What the record actually shows is the opposite.'

parts: the board wanted was hard evidence, not opinion.
was the reliability of the product
won AVRT its clients, not the low price. The
that the upside is real and the risk is small.

gapfill.text: Cleft sentences split one idea into two clauses to EMPHASIZE one part. It-cleft: 'It was the lead time that won the deal' (emphasis on the lead time). What-cleft: 'What convinced the board was the track record' (emphasis on what follows 'was'). The reason ... is that: 'The reason we recommend it is that the upside is real' (never 'is because'). Emphasis makes a written or spoken argument land harder.

bank.label: Useful language for making a case with emphasis
items: What makes the case compelling is the speed.
It is the technology that gives us the edge.
The bottom line is that we would save a year.
The main objection is price, but the valuation can be checked.
What the record shows is that reliability wins.
The reason we recommend it is that the upside is real.

lf.title: Cleft and emphasis: which structure?
items: A
What
impressed the board was the track record.
strong
B
It
was the lead time that won the deal.
strong
C
The
reason
we recommend it is that the upside is real.
soft
D
What
we need now is the board's approval.
strong

lf.text: It-cleft: It + be + emphasized part + that/who + rest ('It was reliability that won them'). What-cleft: What + clause + be + emphasized part ('What won them was reliability'). Use 'The reason ... is that', never 'The reason ... is because'. Cleft and emphasis let you put the most important idea in the spotlight.

items: Scenario 1
Emphasize the deciding factor of the deal with an it-cleft: 'It was ... that ...'.
Scenario 2
Emphasize what the board wanted with a what-cleft: 'What ... was ...'.
Scenario 3
State your recommendation with 'The reason ... is that ...'.
Scenario 4
Make the case for one real decision at work, using at least two cleft or emphatic structures.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = What, 2 = It, 3 = that, 4 = reason, 5 = is.
Scenario 1: 'It was the lead time that won the deal.'
Scenario 2: 'What the board wanted was hard evidence.'
Scenario 3: 'The reason we recommend it is that the upside is real.'
answerkey.note: It-cleft: It + be + emphasized part + that/who. What-cleft: What + clause + be + emphasized part. 'The reason ... is that' -- never 'is because'.
